title: How Neural Networks Detect
concept: Neural networks detect objects by running convolutional layers to extract features and using multiple output heads to predict classes and box coordinates.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main goal of localization in object detection?

options.text: To find the spatial coordinates of the object

exercises.explain: Localization aims to find the specific spatial bounding box coordinates of an object.

cards.title: Looking through a Grid
cards.body: When you look at a painting, you don't just see pixels. You see lines, shapes, and textures that combine into objects. A neural network does something similar — it divides the image into a grid and analyzes each section for objects.

cards.title: Deep Learning Detection
cards.body: Modern object detection uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). They pass the image through convolutional layers to extract feature maps, which are then used by output heads to predict class labels and bounding box offsets.

cards.title: The Sketch Artist
cards.body: Imagine an artist drawing a portrait. The early layers of their brain notice lines and edges. They sketch a rough outline of the face first (localization) and then fill in details like eye color and hair style (classification) to finish the work.

cards.title: YOLO and SSD
cards.body: State-of-the-art models like YOLO (You Only Look Once) or SSD (Single Shot MultiBox Detector) pass an image through a CNN backbone once, predicting classes and coordinates across the entire image in a single pass.

cards.title: Networks don't 'draw' boxes
cards.body: It's a myth that networks output physical colored pixels to draw a box. Instead, they output a set of numbers representing coordinates (like center x, center y, width, and height), and the software draws the lines on the screen.

cards.title: Dual-Head Outputs
cards.body: Visualizing how a single feature extractor feeds two output heads:

right.text:
Predicts box coordinates:
[x=0.2, y=0.3, w=0.4, h=0.5]

exercises.prompt: How does a neural network output the bounding box coordinates?
exercises.explain: The network outputs bounding box coordinates as continuous numbers from a regression head.

options.text: By coloring pixels directly inside the image
By using a regression head that predicts numeric coordinates
By running data augmentation during inference
By converting the image to grayscale

exercises.prompt: True or False: Modern object detection networks must run separate forward passes for classification and localization.
exercises.explain: False. Modern detectors predict both class labels and bounding box coordinates in a single forward pass using shared convolutional features.
The backbone extracts feature maps, which contain high-level spatial and semantic details used for prediction.
exercises.sentence: Object detection models use a convolutional backbone to extract ___ maps from the image.

exercises.prompt: Which component is responsible for predicting the actual class of a detected object?
exercises.explain: The classification head outputs probabilities for each object class.

exercises.prompt: You are building a fast real-time model to detect pedestrians for a drone. To make the model run as fast as possible on hardware, how should you structure the output heads?

options.text: Have a single CNN backbone with two parallel output heads: one for classification and one for coordinate regression, sharing features.
Run two separate CNN backbones: one to find where objects are, and a separate one to classify them.
Run a classical filter on CPU first, then pass it to the CNN.
Only predict classification labels and ignore localization coordinates.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Sharing a convolutional backbone and having parallel heads is the foundation of efficient object detection. Next: let's dive into how we represent bounding boxes mathematically!
